prompt: A rigorous 1957 exhibition poster for the 'Institut für Informatik' in the Swiss International Typographic Style. The composition is governed by a strict 12-column modular grid with mathematical asymmetrical balance. Concept: Support Vector Machine (SVM) binary classification. A thick, 45-degree diagonal black bar (the maximum margin hyperplane) bisects the frame. Parallel to it are two hairline-thin dashed lines defining the margins. The 'support vectors' are primary geometric forms: two solid cadmium-red squares and one solid carbon-black circle, precisely flush with the dashed margin lines. Typography: 'support vector machine' in lowercase Neue Haas Grotesk, set with tight kerning and aligned to the grid's horizontal flow lines. Color palette: Matte white paper, black, and one spot-color red. No gradients, no drop shadows. Textures: subtle offset-lithography ink-bleed and heavy 200gsm paper grain. Everything is flat 2D graphic design.
tradition_approach: I targeted the 'objective' clarity of the 12-column grid and 'Neue Haas Grotesk' typography. The SVM margins are rendered as functional white space, emphasizing the movement's focus on mathematical tension and the reduction of elements to their geometric essence.
